Chunk # 0.
Journal of General Microbiology (1977), 99,291-300 
Printed in Great Britain 
Isolation of Mitochondria and Mitochondria1 R.NA 
from Crithidia fasciculata 
By JOSIE M NICHOLS* AND G A M CROSS 
M.R.C Biochemical Parasitology Unit, University of Cambridge, 
Molten0 Institute, Downing Street, Cambridge CB2 3 EE 
(Received 13 September 1976; revised I November 1976) 
SUMMARY 
Two methods were used to isolate mitochondria from Crithidia fasciculata In 
the first method, cells were weakened by exposure to hypotonic conditions and then 
disrupted by blending; mitochondria were subsequently isolated using disodium 
3,5-diacetoamido-2,4,6triiodobenzoate gradients In the second, cells were 
treated with digitonin before disruption ; mitochondria were purified by differential 
centrifugation Both preparations were examined with the electron microscope and 
were also shown to possess several characteristic biochemical properties of mito- 
chondria Kinetoplast DNA was present in the mitochondria, uncontaminated by 
nuclear DNA 
Analysis by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis showed two RNA components 
of molecular weights 0.47 x IO~ and 0.22 x IO~, in addition to cytoplasmic RNA 
contamination Four mitochondrial components with sedimentation coefficients 
of 14-6s~ 11*4S, 10-1s and 6.9s were identified on sucrose density gradients 
Ethidium bromide abolished the incorporation of [5-SH]uridine into the presumed 
mitoch ondrial RNA 
INTRODUCTION 
Members of the protozoan order Kinetoplastida, which includes the genera Crithidia, 
Leishmania and Trypanosoma, are believed to contain a single mitochondrion (Paulin, 1975) 
If this mitochondrion is equivalent to mitochondria in other organisms, it should contain 
DNA coding for specific mitochondrial RNA and protein The kinetoplast is composed of 
DNA (Bresslau & Scremin, 1924; Steinert, Firket & Steinert, 1958) and is a modified region 
of the single mitochondrion (Steinert, 1960) 
The function of kinetoplast DNA (K-DNA) is unknown, although its presence or absence 
may be coupled to the proliferation and regression of functional mitochondria (Simpson, 
1972) Verification of the presence of RNA encoded by K-DNA in the mitochondrion would 
give support for K-DNA functioning as mitochondrial DNA We decided to approach the 
problem by isolating mitochondria from Crithidia fasciculata, this species being chosen 
because of its relative ease of culture However, the existence of a single mitochondrion 
consisting of several tubular extensions makes it difficult to isolate an intact mitochondrion, 
especially as the kinetoplastid flagellates are relatively resistant to cell breakage by standard 
methods (Simpson, 1968; 1972) Several techniques have previously been used to accomplish 
this (Hill & White, 1968; Renger & Wolstenholme, 1970; Renger & Wolstenholme, 1972; 
Kusel & Storey, 1972; Toner & Weber, 1972; Braly, Simpson & Kretzer, 1974) 
This paper describes the isolation of mitochondria, mitochondrial RNA and mito- 
chondrial (kinetoplast) DNA from C fasciculata 
* Present address: Biochemistry Department, University of Liverpool, P.O Box 147, Liverpool LQ 3BX 
19 MI= 99 292 J M NICHOLS AND G A M CROSS 
METHODS 
Materials Ethidium bromide was obtained from Boots Pure Drug Co., Nottingham; and 
actinomycin D, bovine serum albumin (fraction V), ribonuclease A, deoxyribonuclease I, 
MOPS [3-(N-morpholino)propanesulphonic acid], digitonin, and diethylpyrocarbonate from 
Sigma Pronase was obtained from Calbiochem; [5-3H]uridine (5.0 Ci mmol-l) from The 
Radiochemical Centre, Amersham ; NADP from Boehringer ; lithium dodecyl sulphate from 
Searle; Renografin (disodium 3,5-diacetoamido-2,4,6-triiodobenzoate) from Winthrop 
Laboratories, Surbiton-upon-Thames, Surrey; and Spurr resin from Agar-Aids, Bishop's 
Stortford, Hertfordshire Other chemicals were obtained in analytical grades wherever 
possible 
Cultivation of organisms Crithidia fasciculata was grown axenically in defined medium 
(Kidder & Dutta, 1958) Batch cultures (500ml) were harvested in the mid-exponential 
phase (3 x 10' to 4 x 10' cells ml-l) to prepare total cell RNA, and in the late-exponential 
phase (8 x 10' to g x 10' cells ml-l) to prepare mitochondria 
Escherichia coli (strain MRE~OO) was obtained as a frozen cake from the Microbiological 
Research Establishment, Porton Down, Wiltshire, and was stored at -20 "C 
Isolation of mitochondria 
Method I : hypotonic swelling and purijication by Renografin gradients The cells were 
harvested by centrifuging at 4000 g for 5 min at 4 "C, washed twice with and resuspended 
(at 5 x 108 cells ml-l) in 2 ~M-EDTA/~ m-Tris/HCl buffer prepared in double-distilled 
water and adjusted to pH 8.0 at 4 "C After about 30 min, when the cells had swollen and 
assumed a spherical shape, the cells were disrupted in an MSE homogenizer for 2.5 min at 
full speed; 80 to go yo rupture was achieved Mitochondria were released as swollen vesicles, 
some of which contained kinetoplasts Immediately after disruption, a concentrated sucrose 
solution was added to the lysate to give a final concentration of 0.25 M Swelling of the cells 
and the extent of breakage were routinely monitored by phase contrast microscopy 
The mitochondria were purified on Renografin gradients using the method of Braly et al 
(1974) The gradients were centrifuged for 2 h at 77500 g in a Beckman SW 40 rotor at 
Method 2: treatment with digitonin and purification by dixerential centrifugation

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Isolation of mitochondria
   - Method 1: Hypotonic swelling and purification by Renografin gradients
     - Harvest cells by centrifugation at 4000 g for 5 min at 4 °C
     - Wash cells twice and resuspend in 2 μM-EDTA/μM-Tris/HCl buffer at pH 8.0
     - Allow cells to swell for about 30 min
     - Disrupt cells in an MSE homogenizer for 2.5 min at full speed, achieving 80 to 90% rupture
     - Mitochondria are released as swollen vesicles, some containing kinetoplasts
     - Add concentrated sucrose solution to the lysate to reach a final concentration of 0.25 M
     - Monitor swelling and breakage using phase contrast microscopy
     - Purify mitochondria on Renografin gradients by centrifuging for 2 h at 77500 g in a Beckman SW 40 rotor
   - Method 2: Treatment with digitonin and purification by differential centrifugation
     - No further details provided

**Conclusion:** The article provides detailed steps for Method 1, which involves hypotonic swelling and purification by Renografin gradients. However, Method 2, which involves treatment with digitonin and purification by differential centrifugation, is not described in detail.

summary: Summary 7: The Journal of General Microbiology published a study by JOSIE M NICHOLS and G A M CROSS from the Biochemistry Department at the University of Liverpool. The research focuses on the isolation and characterization of mitochondria in Kinetoplastida, specifically the genera Leishmania and Trypanosoma. The study investigates the function, structure, and biochemical properties of mitochondria, including the analysis of mitochondrial RNA and Kinetoplast DNA. Techniques such as polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis and sucrose density gradients are used to isolate and study these components. The research also examines the proliferation and regression of functional mitochondria and the presence of RNA encoded by Kinetoplast DNA. This study contributes to our understanding of mitochondrial biology in protozoan organisms.

RESULTS 
Properties of the mitochondrial preparations 
After centrifugation in Renografin gradients (method I), two bands could usually be seen 
by eye, although the size of the upper band, containing membrane fragments and flagella in 
addition to mitochondria, was very variable The lower band, at a density of 1-18 g ml-l, 
contained mitochondria with little cell debris and no whole cells (Fig ~a) Smaller mito- 
chondria, of 0.5 pm diameter, had a condensed matrix and large intracristal spaces, whereas 
larger mitochondria, some having a diameter of over ~.opm, had a less electron-dense 
matrix and smaller intracristal spaces Some mitochondria contained a section through a 
kinetoplast The existence of various densities in the matrices could represent various 
degrees of leakiness of the mitochondria Absorbance at 600 nm was used to follow the 
distribution of light-scattering material in the gradients; the distribution of protein and 
succinate dehydrogenase activity coincided with the material at 1.18 g ml-l, with the peak 
activity being 57 nmol succinate oxidized min-l (mg protein)-l

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Disrupt cells in an MSE homogenizer for 1 min at full speed at 4°C.
2. Fix samples in 2.5% glutaraldehyde in 0.25 M-sucrose/0.2 M-cacodylate buffer pH 7.2 for 1 to 2 h at 4°C.
3. Wash samples at 4°C in the same buffer for 15 min and overnight.
4. Embed samples in 2% Difco Bacto-agar in sucrose/cacodylate buffer and cut into small pieces.
5. Wash pieces in sucrose/cacodylate buffer (4 x 15 min).
6. Postfix samples with 2% osmium tetroxide in 0.2 M-cacodylate for 1 h at 0°C.
7. Dehydrate samples in a graded series of ethanol/water mixtures.
8. Infiltrate samples with Spurr resin and embed in fresh Spurr resin.
9. Section samples on an LKB Ultratome 111 and collect on uncoated copper grids.
10. Stain sections in a saturated solution of uranyl acetate in 50% ethanol for 15 min.
11. Stain sections in lead citrate for 10 min.
12. Examine sections in a Philips EM300 electron microscope.

Conclusion: The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation, including cell disruption, fixation, embedding, sectioning, and staining. However, there are some protocol gaps, such as the specific concentration and composition of the buffers used, as well as the details of the sucrose density gradient centrifugation.

summary: The Kusel & Storey (1972) method was used to disrupt cells in an MSE homogenizer. Cell rupture exceeded 80% at 4°C. Electron microscopy was performed after fixing samples in glutaraldehyde and embedding them in agar. RNA was extracted using the Kirby & Parish method, and its concentration was estimated. Polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis was conducted to analyze the RNA samples. Sucrose density gradient centrifugation was used to separate RNA. DNA analysis involved lysing cells or organelles and performing CsCl centrifugation. Enzyme assays were conducted to measure succinate dehydrogenase and glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase activity. The mitochondrial preparations showed two bands in Renografin gradients, with the lower band containing mitochondria and minimal cell debris.

Analysis of mitochondrial RNA by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis 
RNA species present in mitochondria, especially rRNA, are distinct from their cyto- 
plasmic counterparts (Borst, I972), and it was hoped that this would also apply in the case of 
mitochondria1 RNA from C fmciculata However, it was first necessary to identify cyto- 
plasmic RNA 
rRNA extracted from C fusciculata and analysed by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis 
consisted of two major components with molecular weights (relative to E coli rRNA) of 
1-28 x 106 and 0.82 x log (Fig 2) Two minor components with molecular weights of 0.71 x 
106 and 0.56 x xo6 are believed to be breakdown products of the larger rRNA species, due 
to a 'hidden' break in the molecule (Pace, 1973) as in Crithidia oncopelti (Spencer & Cross, 
1976) and Crithidia luciliae (Reijnders et al., 1973) The small high molecular weight peak 
in the position expected for DNA (Loening, 1967) was absent after DNAase treatment of 
the sample 
RNA extracted from mitochondria prepared by digitonin treatment and differential 
centrifugation had two additional components (Fig 2) ; their average molecular weights 
were calculated to be 0-47 x ro6 and 0.22 x ro6 (corresponding to sedimentation coefficients 
of 14.5s and 9'5s) The amount of contamination by cytoplasmic RNA varied from 45 to 
63 74 Treatment of samples with DNAase or RNAase confirmed that all components were 
RNA except for that of the highest molecular weight band which was DNA

summary:
SPR:
- CsCl equilibrium centrifugation showed fast-banding DNA in mitochondria1 lysates, characteristic of kinetoplast DNA.
- Ultraviolet scans of RNA extracted from whole cells and mitochondria revealed a single component of buoyant density 1.703 g ml-l, identified as kinetoplast DNA.
- The final mitochondrial pellet obtained by method 2 contained flagella and cell membranes, but the mitochondria were of uniform size and approximately 0.5 pm diameter.
- Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase activity was detected in extracts of C fcrsciculata, but only 1 to 5% of the activity was found in the final mitochondrial preparations.
- The best mitochondrial preparations were obtained through a combination of digitonin treatment and Renograh centrifugation.
- Mitochondrial RNA from C fmciculata had distinct species, including rRNA, with molecular weights different from cytoplasmic RNA.
- Two major components of rRNA from C fmciculata had molecular weights of 1.28 x 106 and 0.82 x 106 relative to E. coli rRNA.
- RNA extracted from mitochondria had two additional components with molecular weights of 0.47 x 106 and 0.22 x 106, corresponding to sedimentation coefficients of 14.5s and 9.5s.
- The highest molecular weight band in the mitochondrial RNA was DNA, as confirmed by DNAase treatment.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from C. fasciculata:

1. Incorporate [5-3H]uridine into growing cells for 1-5 generations in the presence of 1 pCi ml-1 [5-3H]uridine.
2. Perform sucrose density gradient analysis of total rRNA or RNA isolated from mitochondria.
3. Analyze the RNA at 4°C for 16 hours in 12 ml 15 to 30% sucrose gradients in 20 a-sodium acetate/1 mM-EDTA/qo m-Tris/HCl buffer, pH f-8, containing 0.5% (w/v) lithium dodecyl sulfate.
4. Determine RNA sedimentation coefficients relative to E. coli rRNA run in duplicate gradients.
5. Detect small radioactive peaks not previously detected by absorbance alone.
6. Recover only 0.07% of incorporated [5-3H]uridine in the final mitochondrial preparation.
7. Estimate that mitochondrial RNA represents about 1% of total cellular RNA.
8. Note that RNA could not be detected in mitochondria purified on Renografin gradients.
9. Add diethylpyrocarbonate, an RNAase inhibitor, to mitochondria purified on Renografin gradients, but observe no effect.
10. Investigate the effects of two RNA inhibitors: actinomycin D and ethidium bromide.
11. Use actinomycin D at 50 pg ml-1 to inhibit the incorporation of [5-3H]uridine into total cell RNA of C. fasciculata by 97%.
12. Observe that C. fasciculata may be relatively impermeable to actinomycin D compared to mammalian cells.
13. Use ethidium bromide at 2 pg ml-1 and observe no detectable inhibition of total RNA synthesis.
14. Use higher concentrations of ethidium bromide (20 pg ml-1 and 40 pg ml-1) to inhibit the incorporation of [5-3H]uridine into RNA by 12% and 45%, respectively.
15. Analyze RNA by sucrose density gradient centrifugation since RNA isolated from mitochondrial preparations would not enter polyacrylamide gels.
16. Observe that RNA isolated from mitochondria contains four components of 14.6s, 11-12s, 10.1s, and 6.95s, in addition to cytoplasmic contaminants.
17. Note that ethidium bromide at 10 pg ml-1 abolishes synthesis of presumptive mitochondrial components, while incorporation of [5-3H]uridine into cytoplasmic contaminants is unaffected.
18. Observe that actinomycin D decreases the large rRNA component compared to the control, with a concomitant increase in the breakdown product at 13.9s.
19. Note no definitive effect upon the synthesis of the mitochondrial RNA components.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation from C. fasciculata. The protocol includes steps for incorporating [5-3H]uridine into growing cells, sucrose density gradient analysis, and the effects of RNA inhibitors. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, such as the absence of specific details on the purification method and the lack of information on the specific concentrations and durations of the RNA inhibitors used. These gaps should be addressed to ensure a complete and reproducible protocol.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. There is evidence that kinetoplasts contain RNA. In 1969, Kallinikova stated that the kinetoplast of Trypanosoma cruzi contained RNA, although no experimental evidence was given.
2. Steinert et al (1969) showed by autoradiography and pulse labeling with [3H]uridine that the kinetoplast was a site of RNA synthesis.
3. Ozeki et al (1971) used autoradiography and enzymatic digestion to show that the kinetoplasts of T. cruzi and T. gambiense contained DNA, RNA, and protein.
4. Simpson (1973) demonstrated two RNA species, isolated from mitochondria of L. tarentolae, with sedimentation coefficients of 9s and 12s. The synthesis of these RNA molecules was inhibited by ethidium bromide.
5. The presence of RNA in the mitochondria supports the hypothesis that kinetoplast DNA may be transcribed.
6. Hybridization studies are required to confirm the relationship between the molecular species identified in K-DNA and the isolated RNA.
7. These studies should also determine which molecular species represents the true mitochondrial DNA.

**Conclusion:**
The steps mentioned in the article provide some insights into the isolation of mitochondria from kinetoplasts. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. Further experimental studies and hybridization studies are needed to fully understand the relationship between K-DNA and isolated RNA.
